title: 5.2.3 Graph Traversal and BFS Algorithm
subtitle: Breadth-first search process and extension-ready visualization/code/practice

content.introduction: Breadth-First Search (BFS) explores graph vertices level by level and naturally computes minimum hop counts in unweighted graphs.

sections.title: 5.2.3.1 Key Ideas and Model
sections.content: Define BFS frontier expansion, queue-based processing, and level distance semantics.
examples: Find minimum edge-count path in an unweighted graph
Compute distance levels from a source
Explain queue behavior during frontier expansion

sections.title: 5.2.3.2 Representation and Workflow
sections.content: Describe BFS workflow with enqueue, visited marking, and parent tracking for path reconstruction.
examples: Trace BFS queue states step by step
Record predecessor array for shortest-path tree
Run BFS from all unvisited nodes for full coverage

sections.title: 5.2.3.3 Complexity and Practical Guidance
sections.content: BFS runs in O(V+E) and uses O(V) memory in worst case. Discuss when BFS is preferred over DFS.
examples: Compare BFS memory usage on wide graphs
Prefer BFS when shortest unweighted path is required

keyTakeaways: Use queue and visited control correctly
Compute shortest paths in unweighted graphs
Choose BFS for breadth-first discovery goals
